1:
"The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God." The witnesses note both Mark's distinctive starting-point and the weight of the title. The Latin fathers compare the four openings. Bede sets Mark beside Matthew: "there He is called the Son of David... but here He is called the Son of God," so "that from less things our sense may by degrees mount up to greater," rising "by faith... to the acknowledgment of His divine eternity." Hilary presses the title: Christ "was the Son of God, not in name only, but by His own proper nature... by origin, not by adoption; in truth, not in name; by birth, not by creation." Pseudo-Jerome expounds the word "Gospel" — "good tidings... by which comes the redemption of the faithful."

The grammatical-historical voices fix Mark's character and the title's force. Robertson notes "each writer has a different starting point": Mark "begins with the work of the Baptist," as "the earliest form of the evangelic tradition," the word "Gospel" here coming "close to meaning the record itself." On the textual question of "Son of God," he holds that if Mark wrote it "there is no reason to doubt the genuineness since he uses the phrase elsewhere." Ellicott connects the title, "in the very title of his Gospel," with "the Apostle's confession in Matthew 16:16," given that Mark reproduces Peter's teaching. Barnes: the title "was used to attract attention... It does not recount the deeds of a hero or philosopher, but the doctrines and doings of the Son of God."

The puritan Henry gathers the riches: it is "gospel — it is God's word, and is faithful and true"; "the gospel of Jesus Christ, the anointed Saviour, the Messiah"; and "this Jesus is the Son of God. That truth is the foundation on which the gospel is built." He, with Barnes and Clarke, recounts Mark's identity — the John Mark of Acts 12:12, sister's son to Barnabas (Colossians 4:10), Peter's "son" (1 Peter 5:13) and interpreter, who "wrote at Rome" "under the eye of Peter."

The Reformed Calvin explains why Mark begins with John: "the Law and the Prophets then came to an end" (John 1:17; Luke 16:16), and Malachi 3:1 "lays down an express distinction between the Law and the new order"; "the abrogation of the Law, strictly speaking, took place when John began to preach." Cyril of Jerusalem adds that the Baptist was "the end of the Prophets" and "the first-fruit of the Gospel history." The witnesses agree: Mark opens not with genealogy or infancy but with the Gospel itself, proclaiming at once that its subject is the Son of God.
2:
"As it is written in the prophets, Behold, I send my messenger before thy face, which shall prepare thy way before thee." The witnesses note that Mark cites two prophets — Malachi 3:1 and Isaiah 40:3 — and they wrestle with the textual question of whether the better reading is "in the prophets" or "in Isaiah the prophet." The Latin fathers resolve the apparent difficulty. Bede: though the first words "are not found in Isaiah, still the sense of them is found... most clearly in this which he has subjoined, The voice of one crying"; he conjectures Mark would have corrected a slip "when reminded," "unless he thought that, since his memory was then ruled by the Holy Spirit, it was not without a purpose." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary holds Mark "compressed into one, two prophecies... and refers them here to Isaiah, who was the first to intimate the sense." Bede expounds John as both "angel" (the Greek for "messenger") "by the dignity of his office," and "voice," since "the voice sounds first, in order that the word may afterwards be heard" — Christ being "the Word of the Father."

The grammatical-historical voices weigh the manuscripts. Lightfoot defends "in the prophets" on three grounds, chiefly that "it is without all example, when two testimonies are taken out of two prophets, to name only the last." Clarke and Ellicott, by contrast, prefer "in Isaiah" as the harder and better-attested reading, Ellicott noting the general reference was likely "specialised by a transcriber" rather than the reverse, and that this is "the only quotation from a prophet made by the Evangelist himself in this Gospel" — a silence partly explained by Mark's writing for Gentiles. Robertson: "Isaiah is mentioned as the chief of the prophets," and it was common to combine prophetic quotations in chains.

Theophylact reads "the way" as "the New Testament" and "the paths" as "the Old," which "should be straightened." Pseudo-Jerome takes "the way of the Lord" as the way "by which He comes into men, in penitence." Gill and Barnes simply identify the two sources, the words being "the words of the Father to the Son, concerning John." The witnesses agree: Mark opens his Gospel with prophetic testimony to John as the messenger who prepares Christ's way, the chief debate being only whether the citation is ascribed to "the prophets" generally or to Isaiah as their head.
3:
"The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the Lord, make his paths straight." The witnesses, here mostly grammatical and devotional, refer the reader to the parallel in Matthew 3:3 (Isaiah 40:3) and dwell on the figure of the herald's voice. Robertson gives the historical sense: "God is coming to his people to deliver them from their captivity in Babylon. So the prophet cries like a voice in the wilderness to make ready for the coming of God." He notes that when the Sanhedrin's committee asked John who he was, "he used this very language of Isaiah (John 1:23). He was only a voice, but we can still hear the echo of that voice through the corridor of the centuries." On "make his paths straight," Robertson recalls "the wonderful Persian roads for the couriers of the king" and the roads that "knit together" the Roman Empire — "John had a high and holy mission as the forerunner of the Messiah."

Gill, Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott all cross-reference Matthew 3 and Isaiah 40:3, treating the verse as the second of the two prophetic testimonies (the first from Malachi, v.2) that establish John as Christ's harbinger. The "wilderness" is the place of the cry — the literal desert of Judea where John preached, and, in the fathers' reading (carried from v.2), the spiritual desolation of a people "deserted by the Spirit of God."

The image of the herald's voice carries the whole weight of John's mission. As Bede had said (under v.2), "the voice sounds first, in order that the word may afterwards be heard" — John the voice, Christ the Word. The voice does not draw attention to itself but to the One it announces; it cries in order that a way may be prepared, and then it falls silent when the Word is heard. So John would later say, "He must increase, but I must decrease" (John 3:30).

The "preparing of the way" and "making straight the paths," the witnesses note, are accomplished by the preaching of repentance: the crooked ways of the heart are straightened, the obstacles of sin removed, that Christ may enter. Cyril of Jerusalem, here on-topic, joins the prophet's cry to the gospel summons — "Prepare ye the way of the Lord... your redemption hath drawn nigh." The witnesses agree: the verse identifies John as the fulfilment of Isaiah's prophesied voice, whose sole office was to make ready, in the wilderness of a sinful world and a sinful heart, the way of the coming Lord.
4:
"John did baptize in the wilderness, and preach the baptism of repentance for the remission of sins." The witnesses agree John's baptism prepared the way but could not itself remit sins. The Latin fathers draw the careful distinction. Bede: John "could not give baptism for the remission of sins... It is therefore only said, Preaching the baptism of repentance for the remission of sins; for he preached a baptism which could remit sins, since he could not give it" — being "the forerunner" by his baptism as by his word. Theophylact agrees: "The baptism of John had not remission of sins, but only brought men to penitence... that they who in penitence received Christ, might receive Him to the remission of their sins." Pseudo-Jerome and Bede expound the camel's hair, leathern girdle, and locusts as marks of "an austere garb" and "mortification" (Galatians 5:24), Bede adding that John "did not encourage the life of sinners by flattery, but chid them by the vigour of his rough rebuke."

The grammatical-historical voices probe the phrase "for (eis) the remission of sins." Robertson: "Certainly John did not mean that the baptism was the means of obtaining the forgiveness of their sins... Probably with reference to is as good a translation as possible. The baptism was on the basis of the repentance and confession of sin," a picture (as Paul explained, Romans 6:4) "of the death to sin and resurrection to new life." He notes the symbol "was already in use by the Jews for proselytes," John "treating the Jewish nation as pagans who need to repent." Ellicott: the phrase means "the sign of repentance... and we cannot doubt that then, as evermore, repentance was followed by forgiveness, even though the blood which availed for that forgiveness had not as yet been shed." Clarke: "Repentance prepared the soul for it, and Baptism was the type or pledge of it." Ellicott marks Mark's abruptness: "No other Gospel passes so... in medias res into the actual work of the Forerunner... St. Mark is here, as elsewhere, emphatically the Evangelist of action."

The witnesses agree: John's baptism was a baptism of repentance, preparing men for the remission that Christ alone would give. It pointed beyond itself — the sign that presupposed repentance and looked forward to forgiveness, and the herald's ordinance that prepared the way for the greater baptism of the Spirit (v.8).
5:
"And there went out unto him all the land of Judea, and they of Jerusalem, and were all baptized of him in the river of Jordan, confessing their sins." The witnesses note the great concourse and the confession that accompanied the baptism. Robertson catches the vividness of Mark's tenses: "the imperfect indicative describing the steady stream of people who kept coming to the baptism" — "a wonderful sight." The public confession "produced a profound impression as they would now." Ellicott observes Mark's care to write "in the river of Jordan" for "those who were not familiar with the topography of Palestine."

The confession of sins was, the witnesses note, the necessary accompaniment of John's baptism. Clarke draws the parallel with proselyte baptism: "it was an invariable custom among the Jews to admit no proselyte to baptism, till he had, in the most solemn manner, declared that he forever had renounced all idolatrous worship... and promised an entire and unreserved submission to the law." Barnes anchors the confession in the Old Testament pattern of penitence (Leviticus 26:40-42; Psalms 32:5; Proverbs 28:13; 1 John 1:8-10) — the open acknowledgment of sin being the mark of true repentance.

Cyril of Jerusalem, here on-topic, draws out the order: "First they shewed their wounds, then he applied the remedies" — confession preceding cleansing, as the patient must lay bare the disease before the physician heals it. He turns it to exhortation, recalling John's "O generation of vipers, who hath warned you to flee from the wrath to come?": "Be not henceforth a viper, but... put off the slough of thy former sinful life."

The witnesses agree: the multitudes streaming out from Judea and Jerusalem, confessing their sins as they were baptized, fulfilled the prophecy that John would "prepare the way" (vv.2-3). The confession was no mere form but the visible turning of a people from sin, the necessary preparation for receiving the Christ whom John announced. The vast response — "all the land of Judea" — both displays the genuine hunger of the people for the promised salvation and prepares, by the herald's labour, a people made ready for the Lord. Yet it also stands, in the witnesses' reading, as a sober foil to the later rejection: many who confessed at Jordan would not, in the end, receive the One to whom John pointed.
6:
"And John was clothed with camel's hair, and with a girdle of a skin about his loins; and he did eat locusts and wild honey." The witnesses read John's dress and diet as the mark of an austere, penitential life befitting the forerunner. Gill notes "it was usual for penitents, and men of austere lives, and of the first class for holiness and religion, to live in deserts, to fare hard, and wear coarse apparel." Robertson clarifies the material: "it was, of course, not camel's skin, but rough cloth woven of camel's hair," and the diet — "dried locusts are considered palatable and the wild honey... was bountiful in the clefts of the rocks." Lightfoot, with rabbinic learning, discusses "camel's wool" or hair as a recognized (if humble) garment material.

The fathers (carried from v.4) read the austere garb spiritually. Bede: John "used a dress more austere than was usual, because he did not encourage the life of sinners by flattery"; the girdle of skin marked him as one "who crucified his flesh with the affections and lusts" (Galatians 5:24); the locusts and honey were "suited to a dweller in the wilderness," his object "not the deliciousness of meats, but the satisfying of the necessity of human flesh." Theophylact found in the camel-hair "grief," for "he who repented should mourn," and in the locusts (which leap up and fall) and wild honey (from bees, that is, the prophets) figures of the Jewish people, who "had the Scriptures... but did not rightly understand them."

The dress also recalled Elijah, "a hairy man, girt with a girdle of leather about his loins" (2 Kings 1:8) — marking John as the promised Elijah who should come (Malachi 4:5). The witnesses thus see in John's whole manner of life a sermon: his self-denial, his contempt of luxury, his contentment with the wilderness's coarse fare, all proclaimed the repentance he preached and prepared men to receive the One mightier than he.

Henry (under v.1) had drawn the lesson: "the more we sit loose to the body, and live above the world, the better we are prepared for Jesus Christ." The witnesses agree: John's rough garment and humble food were not incidental but expressive — the visible token of a life wholly given to his prophetic calling, and a rebuke to the soft raiment and self-indulgence of those who would not hear his cry. The forerunner's austerity matched his message, and both prepared the way of the Lord.
7:
"And preached, saying, There cometh one mightier than I after me, the latchet of whose shoes I am not worthy to stoop down and unloose." The witnesses fix on John's self-effacing humility before Christ. Gill: "John was a preacher of Jesus Christ; of the dignity of his person, the excellency of his office." Bede (under v.4) had noted John proclaimed the Lord "not yet as God, or the Son of God, but only as a man mightier than himself," because "his ignorant hearers were not yet capable of receiving" the full mystery, "but gradually... were to be introduced to the belief of His Divine Eternity."

The grammatical-historical voices catch the force of the menial image, distinctive to Mark. Ellicott notes the difference from Matthew's "whose shoes I am not worthy to bear": here it is "the latchet of whose shoes I am not worthy to stoop down and unloose" — the "latchet" being "the thong or lace with which shoes or sandals were fastened." "To stoop down and loosen the sandals was commonly the act of the servant who afterwards carried them, but it expressed more vividly... the menial character of the office, and therefore was chosen by St. Mark." Robertson: "the thong of the sandal which held it together," loosed "by a slave before one enters the bath. Mark alone gives this touch." He judges John's self-depreciation "sincere," John giving "a reason for it."

The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary draws out the height of Christ's dignity: John says this "to extol the excellence of the power of Christ... as if he said, Not even in the station of his servant am I worthy to be reckoned." Gregory adds the marriage-custom — that he who declined a kinswoman had his shoe loosed — so John "proclaims himself unworthy to loose his shoe-latchet... I usurp not the name of the Bridegroom." The Gloss notes the saying was meant "to do away with the opinion of the crowd, who thought that he was the Christ."

The witnesses agree: John, "though one of the greatest that was born of women" (Henry), thought himself unworthy of the meanest slave's office about Christ. His humility magnified the One he announced; the lower he placed himself, the higher he exalted Christ. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment here, on "Come unto me," belongs to Matthew 11, not this verse.) The forerunner's self-abasement is, for the witnesses, the pattern of all true ministry — to decrease that Christ may increase.
8:
"I indeed have baptized you with water: but he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost." The witnesses read this as John's pointing beyond his own baptism to the greater baptism of Christ. Gill: John spoke this "partly to take off their dependence upon him and his baptism; and partly to direct their views to Christ, from whom the gifts and graces of the Spirit are alone to be had." Clarke: "This baptism is not to be rested in; it is only an emblem of that which you must receive from him who is mightier than I. It is he only who can communicate the Holy Spirit; and water baptism is nothing, but as it points out, and leads to, the baptism of the Holy Ghost."

The grammatical-historical voices note Mark's omission and the contrast. Ellicott observes that "St. Mark omits the fire which St. Matthew joins with the Holy Ghost, possibly as less intelligible to his Gentile readers." Robertson marks the prepositions: Mark and Luke have "with water" (the locative, "in water"), Matthew has "in water and the Holy Spirit"; and "the water baptism by John was a symbol of the spiritual baptism by Jesus." Barnes cross-references the fulfilment at Pentecost and after (Joel 2:28; Acts 1:5; 2:4; 10:45; 1 Corinthians 12:13).

Jerome (under v.4) had drawn the essential contrast: "What is the difference between water and the Holy Ghost, who was borne over the face of the waters? Water is the ministry of man; but the Spirit is ministered by God." This is the heart of the verse for the witnesses: John's baptism, however solemn, was an outward and human ordinance; Christ's baptism is inward and divine, the very gift of the Holy Spirit. That Christ can baptize with the Spirit is itself a proof of his deity, for, as Bede said (under v.4), "who can doubt that none other but God can give the grace of the Holy Ghost?"

Cyril of Jerusalem, on-topic, explains the "fire" (which Mark omits): "Because the descent of the Holy Ghost was in fiery tongues" (Acts 2), recalling Christ's word, "I am come to send fire on the earth." The witnesses agree: John drew a sharp line between his preparatory water-baptism and the Spirit-baptism of the mightier One — the sign and the substance, the herald's ordinance and the gift only God can give. John's whole ministry was to lead men from the one to the other.
9:
"And it came to pass in those days, that Jesus came from Nazareth of Galilee, and was baptized of John in Jordan." The witnesses ask why the sinless Christ submitted to a baptism of repentance, and they unfold the Trinitarian theophany that followed. The Latin fathers give the reasons. The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary: Christ "was baptized that He might be made known to all... and fulfil all righteousness," and "that from heaven sanctification might be given to men, and earthly things be joined to heavenly." Bede: "He was baptized, that by being baptized Himself He might shew His approval of John's baptism, and that, by sanctifying the waters of Jordan through the descent of the dove, He might shew the coming of the Holy Ghost in the laver of believers." Bede draws out the "mystery of the Trinity": "the Son is baptized, the Spirit comes down in the shape of a dove, the voice of the Father bearing witness to the Son is heard."

The puritan Henry: "See how humbly he owned God, by coming to be baptized of John... he took upon him the likeness of sinful flesh, that, though he was perfectly pure... he was washed as if he had been polluted; and thus for our sakes he sanctified himself" (John 17:19). And "see how honourably God owned him" — the opened heavens, the descending Spirit, the Father's voice.

The grammatical-historical voices note Mark's distinctive touches. Ellicott: "St. Mark adds from Nazareth to St. Matthew's more general statement, from Galilee." Robertson marks Mark's vivid "rent asunder" for the heavens (v.10) and observes that the Baptist saw the Spirit descend (John 1:32); he warns against the Cerinthian Gnostic reading that made the dove a heavenly "Christ" descending on the man Jesus. Gill insists it was Christ, not John, who came up out of the water.

Pseudo-Chrysostom guards against misunderstanding the Spirit's descent: it was "not as if He then first came to Him, for He never had left Him; but that He might shew forth the Christ... and point Him out to all, as it were by the finger of faith." The witnesses agree: the sinless Christ was baptized not for his own sin but to fulfil all righteousness, sanctify the waters, identify with sinners, and be openly manifested as the Son — the whole Trinity bearing witness at the threshold of his ministry.
10:
"And straightway coming up out of the water, he saw the heavens opened, and the Spirit like a dove descending upon him." The brief verse draws short comment, but the witnesses fasten on Mark's vivid language and the descent of the Spirit. Ellicott marks the boldness of Mark's word: "Better, as in the margin, rent open, St. Mark's language here, as elsewhere, being more boldly vivid than that of the other Gospels." Where Matthew and Luke say the heavens were "opened," Mark says they were split — as Robertson put it (under v.9), "split like a garment," a more dramatic image. Gill insists the one who came up out of the water and saw the heavens was Christ himself, not John.

The descent of the Spirit "like a dove" the fathers (carried from v.9) read richly. Bede: "Well indeed in the shape of a dove did the Holy Ghost come down, for it is an animal of great simplicity, and far removed from the malice of gall, that in a figure He might shew us that He looks out for simple hearts, and deigns not to dwell in the minds of the wicked." And Bede stressed "abiding on Him" (the fuller reading): "this is peculiar to Christ, that the Holy Ghost once filling Him should never leave Him" — unlike the disciples, to whom the Spirit's gifts for miracles are sometimes given and sometimes withdrawn.

The rent heavens and the descending dove together mark the inauguration of Christ's public ministry with a visible sign of the Spirit's anointing. Henry (under v.9) had drawn the application: "Then we may see heaven opened to us, when we perceive the Spirit descending and working upon us. God's good work in us is the surest evidence of his good will towards us."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which forms the centre of the baptismal theophany (vv.9-11): the heavens torn open, the Spirit descending as a dove, and (v.11) the Father's voice. The witnesses see in the dove both the gentleness and simplicity that the Spirit loves and rests upon, and the permanent anointing that distinguishes Christ — the Spirit coming not to depart but to abide, equipping the incarnate Son for the work of redemption he now begins. The same Spirit, the witnesses note, will "immediately" (v.12) drive him into the wilderness to be tempted, so that the anointing for ministry is at once an anointing for conflict.
11:
"And there came a voice from heaven, saying, Thou art my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased." The witnesses note the Father's testimony and the form of address. Gill identifies it with what "the Jews call Bath Kol," the daughter of a voice from heaven. Robertson marks the difference of form among the Gospels: "Thou art (su ei). So Luke 3:22. Matthew 3:17 has this is (houtos estin)... So both Mark and Luke have in thee, while Matthew has in whom." Augustine (under v.9) had reconciled them: "the Evangelists, though not relating the same form of speaking, relate the same meaning" — Matthew's "This is my Son" addressed to the bystanders that they might know, Mark and Luke's "Thou art" addressed to the Son.

Barnes cross-references Psalm 2:7, the Messianic decree, "Thou art my Son." The voice declares both Christ's unique Sonship and the Father's delight in him. Bede (under v.9) guarded against misreading: the voice did not inform "the Son Himself of a thing of which He was ignorant, but it shews to us what we ought to believe" — and teaches "that we also, by the water of cleansing, and by the Spirit of sanctification, may be made the sons of God."

Cyril of Jerusalem, on-topic, presses the verse against the Jews who deny the Son: "The Father spake with a loud voice from heaven, saying, This is My beloved Son... unless thou also be well pleased in Him, thou hast not life. Be not carried away with the Jews when they craftily say, There is one God alone; but with the knowledge that God is One, know that there is also an Only-begotten Son of God" — citing Psalm 2:7, "The Lord said unto Me, Thou art My Son."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which completes the baptismal theophany. The witnesses agree: the Father's voice from heaven sets the seal of divine approval on the Son at the outset of his ministry, declaring him the beloved Son in whom the Father delights. Henry (under v.9) drew out the double comfort: God "loved him never the less for that low and mean estate to which he had now humbled himself," and "loved him much the more for that glorious and kind undertaking in which he had now engaged himself" — "so well pleased in him, as to be well pleased with us in him." The whole Trinity thus attests the Son as he steps forward as Redeemer.
12:
"And immediately the Spirit driveth him into the wilderness." The witnesses fasten on Mark's strong word "driveth" and its theological import. The Antiochene Chrysostom reads it as a lesson for the baptized: Christ "began after His baptism to dwell in the wilderness, and fought against the devil, that every baptized person might patiently sustain greater temptations after His baptism, nor be troubled." The word "driven" rather than merely "went" shows "that it was done according to the word of Divine Providence... that no man should thrust himself into temptation, but that those who from some other state are as it were driven into temptation, remain conquerors." The devil "approaches not save where he has beheld one set in a place of greater honour."

Bede guards against misreading the "Spirit": following Luke, who says Christ was "full of the Spirit" and "led by the Spirit," lest "the evil spirit should be thought to have any power over Him." He retires "that He may teach us that, leaving the allurements of the world... we should in all things obey the Divine commands," and is tempted "that He might teach us that all that will live godly in Christ Jesus shall suffer persecution" (2 Timothy 3:12).

The grammatical-historical voices stress the vividness. Robertson: "Driveth him forth... Vivid word, bolder than Matthew's was led up and Luke's was led. It is the same word employed in the driving out of demons." Barnes clarifies the sense: "The word driveth does not mean that he was compelled forcibly against his will... but that he was inclined to go there by the Spirit, or was led there. The Spirit of God, for important purposes, caused him to go." Clarke renders it "putteth him forth." Ellicott notes Mark's characteristic "immediately" and the "stronger word driveth him."

Wesley draws the lesson: "in all the children of God, extraordinary manifestations of his favour are wont to be followed by extraordinary temptations." The witnesses agree: the same Spirit who descended at the baptism (v.10) immediately impels Christ into the wilderness for conflict with Satan. The anointing for ministry is at once an anointing for battle; and the strong word "driveth" marks both the divine purpose behind the temptation and the truth that we do not seek temptation, but, when God leads us into it, are made conquerors in it.
13:
"And he was there in the wilderness forty days, tempted of Satan; and was with the wild beasts; and the angels ministered unto him." The witnesses note that Mark compresses the temptation, omitting its three forms, but adds the distinctive detail of the wild beasts. Ellicott: "St. Mark compresses the history by omitting the several forms of the Temptation. Peculiar to him are (1) the use of Satan instead of the devil; (2) the statement that Jesus was with the wild beasts" — which "in our Lord's time might include the panther, the bear, the wolf, the hyena, possibly the lion." He reads the detail two ways: "partly that their presence added to the terrors of the Temptation, partly that in His being protected from them there was the fulfilment of the promise in the very Psalm which furnished the Tempter with his chief weapon" — that the child of God should trample "the lion and the adder" (Psalm 91:13).

Robertson agrees: "It was the haunt at night of the wolf, the boar, the hyena, the jackal, the leopard. It was lonely and depressing in its isolation and even dangerous." He notes Swete's observation that in Psalm 91:13 "the promise of victory over the wild beasts comes immediately after that of angelic guardianship cited by Satan." Lightfoot: "He was among the wild beasts, but was not touched by them. So Adam first before his fall." Clarke sees the wild beasts as "emblematical of that savage and brutal cruelty with which he was persecuted to death."

The puritan Henry (under v.9) had drawn out both the temptation and the comfort: "It was an instance of his Father's care of him, that he was preserved from being torn in pieces by the wild beasts... Special protections are earnests of seasonable supplies." And "the serpent tempted the first Adam in the garden, the Second Adam in the wilderness; with different success indeed." The angels' ministry, Bede (under v.12) and the Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary noted, came after the victory (Hebrews 1:14): "to those who conquer in temptation angels stand near and minister."

The witnesses agree: in the desolate wilderness, amid wild beasts and Satan's assault, the Second Adam stood where the first fell, was preserved as the true child of God (Psalm 91), and was ministered to by angels after his victory. Mark's spare account fixes attention on the bare fact of the forty-day conflict and its outcome — the Conqueror prepared, by trial, for the ministry he now begins.
14:
"Now after that John was put in prison, Jesus came into Galilee, preaching the gospel of the kingdom of God." The witnesses mark the transition from John's ministry to Christ's, and the content of his preaching. The Latin and Antiochene voices note the timing and the retirement. The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary: after the temptation "He goes back into Galilee, teaching us not to resist the violence of evil men," and "since the time was fulfilled... it was fitting that the race of man should obtain the last dispensation of God." Theophylact draws the double lesson: "in persecutions we ought to retire, and not to await them; but when we fall into them, we must sustain them"; and "the time of the Law is complete... from this time the kingdom of God will work, that is, a conversation according to the Gospel."

The puritan Henry: Christ "began to preach in Galilee, after that John was put in prison... When John was silenced, Christ began to preach; for when it is in the heart of any to do good, God can find them work, and put them into a capacity of doing it." The preaching of the kingdom takes up and advances what John began.

The grammatical-historical voices weigh the wording. Barnes and Robertson note Mark's "the gospel of the kingdom of God" — the good news that God's reign was now at hand. Ellicott observes Mark's "kingdom of God" where Matthew has "kingdom of heaven." Robertson marks that Jesus came "preaching" (heralding) the good news, taking up his public ministry after John's arrest.

The Reformed Calvin connects it to the larger structure: this marks the commencement of the proper Gospel ministry, John's imprisonment closing the preparatory work and Christ's preaching opening the new dispensation. The witnesses agree: with the forerunner silenced, Christ comes forward into Galilee as the preacher of the kingdom of God, the long-awaited reign now drawing near. The arrest of John is not a defeat but a handing-on: the voice falls silent as the Word begins to speak. And Christ's withdrawal to Galilee, even as he advances the gospel, teaches (with the fathers) both prudence in the face of persecution and steadfastness when it comes — the kingdom advancing not by resisting violence but by the proclamation of the good news.
15:
"And saying, The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand: repent ye, and believe the gospel." The witnesses read this as the summary of Christ's preaching — the announcement of the appointed time and the call to repentance and faith. Ellicott notes the words "are not found in the parallel passages of the other Gospels, and are interesting as embodying the same thought as St. Paul's in the fulness of time (Galatians 4:4; Ephesians 1:10)." Mark also "adds believe the gospel to the simple repent of St. Matthew, and gives the kingdom of God instead of the kingdom of heaven."

On "the time is fulfilled," Gill explains it is "the time fixed for the end of the law... or the fulness of time for the Messiah's appearance," predicted in prophecy and expected by the Jews. Clarke ties it to Daniel: "the time appointed for sending the Messiah; and particularly the time specified by Daniel" (Daniel 9:24-27). He draws four points: that all is "done according to a plan laid by the Divine wisdom"; that the kingdom of sin is to be destroyed and the kingdom of grace established; that this reign "begins with repentance for past sins"; and that "this reign of grace is at hand." Wesley: "The time of my kingdom, foretold by Daniel, expected by you, is fully come."

The double command — "repent ye, and believe the gospel" — the witnesses take as the proper response to the kingdom's nearness. Barnes: "Repent ye — exercise sorrow for sins, and turn from them. And believe the Gospel — literally, trust in the gospel, or believe the good tidings respecting salvation." Repentance and faith are joined as the two-fold turning required of all who would enter the kingdom.

The thin comment matches the compact verse, which the witnesses recognize as a kind of epitome of the whole Gospel message. The witnesses agree: Christ's opening proclamation declares that the long-appointed hour has come, the reign of God is breaking in, and the only fit response is to turn from sin and trust the good news. The "fulness of time" (Galatians 4:4) had arrived; the kingdom so long foretold and awaited was now at hand; and the door into it stands open to all who will repent and believe. Clarke's closing note gathers the urgency: "now is the accepted time to enter in."
16:
"Now as he walked by the sea of Galilee, he saw Simon and Andrew his brother casting a net into the sea: for they were fishers." The witnesses note the call of the first disciples and harmonize it with John's account of an earlier meeting. Theophylact explains: "Peter and Andrew were disciples of the Forerunner, but... afterwards, grieving that John had been cast into prison, they returned to their trade." So "He calls them for the second time; for this is the second calling in respect of that of which we read in John." He commends their honest labour: "living on their own labours, not on the fruits of iniquity; for such men were worthy to become the first disciples of Christ." Bede harmonizes Mark with Luke 5, arguing the great catch of fish came first, after which "they, as their custom was, had returned to their fishing," so that "what Mark here relates happened afterwards."

The fathers read the calling of fishers spiritually. Remigius: "by the net of holy preaching they drew fish, that is, men, from the depths of the sea... to the light of faith. Wonderful indeed is this fishing! for fishes when they are caught, soon after die; when men are caught by the word of preaching, they rather are made alive." Bede: "fishers and unlettered men are sent to preach, that the faith of believers might be thought to lie in the power of God, not in eloquence or in learning."

The grammatical-historical voices supply the scene. Robertson catches Mark's realism: "Mark uses para (along, beside) twice and makes the picture realistic. He catches this glimpse of Christ in action," and explains the net-casting verb — "literally casting on both sides, now on one side, now on the other... fishing with a net, making a cast." The four — Simon, Andrew, James, John — "were fishermen and were partners" (Luke 5:7). Ellicott notes Mark "names Simon without the addition of Peter."

The witnesses agree: Christ, walking by the familiar sea, calls working fishermen — humble men of honest toil, already prepared by John's ministry and an earlier meeting — to become the first disciples and the future preachers of his gospel. The choice of unlettered fishers, the fathers note, magnifies the power of God in the spread of the faith: that the world should be won not by human eloquence but by men whom Christ would make "fishers of men" (v.17).
17:
"And Jesus said unto them, Come ye after me, and I will make you to become fishers of men." The brief verse draws short but pointed comment. Gill paraphrases the call: "Leave your worldly employments, and become my disciples." Robertson fastens on the verb Mark uniquely uses, "become": "Mark has this word not in Matthew. It would be a slow and long process, but Jesus could and would do it. He would undertake to make fishers of men out of fishermen. Preachers are made out of laymen who are willing to leave their business for service for Christ."

The promise "I will make you to become" is, for the witnesses, the heart of the verse. Christ does not call men already qualified; he calls fishermen and undertakes himself to make them into fishers of men. The qualification is his gift, not their attainment — a slow, patient process of training under his hand. Theophylact (under v.16) had drawn the urgency of the response: "We must not allow any time to lapse, but at once follow the Lord," and the spiritual image (with Remigius) of the new fishing, in which men "caught by the word of preaching" are "made alive."

The play on their trade is deliberate. They had spent their lives drawing fish from the sea; Christ would make them draw men from the depths of sin and unbelief into the light of the gospel. The skills of patience, persistence, and labour learned at the nets would be transfigured into the apostolic work of winning souls. The metaphor, the witnesses note, both honours the disciples' former calling and elevates it: the same diligence is now turned to an eternal harvest.

The thin comment matches the brevity of the verse, which records Christ's word of call. The witnesses agree on its abiding significance: Christ summons ordinary working men from their trade to follow him, and promises to make them what they cannot make themselves — fishers of men. The call is at once a command ("Come after me") and a promise ("I will make you"); and the whole subsequent history of the apostles is the unfolding of that promise, as the Lord, by patient discipling, formed fishermen into the preachers who would draw the nations into the net of the gospel. Every call to follow Christ, the witnesses imply, carries the same promise — that he who calls will also equip.
18:
"And straightway they forsook their nets, and followed him." The brief verse records the immediate obedience of Simon and Andrew, and the witnesses dwell on its promptness and cost. Gill notes the nets "perhaps were their all" — the whole of their livelihood, left at once at Christ's word. Wesley: "From this time they forsook their employ, and constantly attended him. Happy they who follow Christ at the first call!"

The word "straightway" (Mark's characteristic adverb, occurring nine times in this first chapter) marks the readiness of their response. There was no delay, no bargaining, no asking for time to settle their affairs. At the call of Christ they left their nets — the instruments of their trade, perhaps their only possession — and followed. The promptness is itself a testimony to the authority of the One who called and to the prepared hearts of those who answered, who (as Theophylact noted under v.16) had already been disciples of John and had met Jesus before.

The cost, though their "all" was modest, was real. As Henry observed of the apostles elsewhere, "they left all for Christ; so far as that what they had should not hinder them from him." The fishermen surrendered their means of living, their security, their familiar work, to follow a Master who had nowhere to lay his head. The smallness of what they left does not lessen the greatness of the surrender, for it was all they had; and the willingness to leave it at once, without hesitation, is the mark of true discipleship.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree on its lesson: the proper response to Christ's call is immediate, whole-hearted obedience. Simon and Andrew did not weigh the cost or postpone the decision; they "straightway" forsook their nets and followed. Their prompt obedience stands as a pattern for all who hear Christ's summons — to follow at the first call, leaving whatever would hinder, trusting the One who calls to provide and to make of them what he has promised (v.17). The blessing Wesley pronounces rests on such ready followers: "Happy they who follow Christ at the first call!" — for delay, the witnesses imply, is the seed of refusal, and the heart that hesitates at the threshold often turns back.
19:
"And when he had gone a little further thence, he saw James the son of Zebedee, and John his brother, who also were in the ship mending their nets." The brief verse records the call of the second pair of brothers, and the witnesses note Mark's characteristic detail. Gill marks the location: "from the place where Simon and Andrew were casting their nets, though still by the sea side." Robertson observes the touches peculiar to Mark: "A little further — a Marcan detail," and "mending their nets" (katartizontas) — "getting ready that they might succeed better at the next haul."

The detail that James and John were "mending their nets" while Simon and Andrew had been "casting" a net (v.16) shows the brothers at the ordinary, patient work of their trade. Mending the nets — repairing the breaks, making them ready — is the unglamorous labour that precedes the catch. Christ found them, as he found the first pair, not idle but diligently at work, faithful in their daily calling. It was to such men, busy and industrious, that the call came.

Theophylact (under v.16) had noted that James and John, "though poor, supported the old age of their father," and that when called "they left their father, because he would have hindered them in following Christ." The mending of the nets in the ship, with their father Zebedee and the hired servants (v.20), pictures the family business they were about to leave. The contemplative John and the active disposition of the brothers were both to be claimed for Christ's service.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which simply records Christ's seeing and approaching the sons of Zebedee. The witnesses agree on its place in the narrative: Christ, having called Simon and Andrew, goes "a little further" and calls James and John, completing the first four of the Twelve. The repeated pattern — fishermen found at their work, called from it to follow — underscores that Christ chooses ordinary, industrious men for the highest service, and that the call to discipleship comes to men in the midst of their daily labour. The diligence shown in mending nets would soon be turned, under Christ's training, to the patient work of building his Church. As with the first pair, the response (v.20) would be immediate, the brothers leaving net, ship, and father to follow the Master who called them.
20:
"And straightway he called them: and they left their father Zebedee in the ship with the hired servants, and went after him." The witnesses note the prompt obedience and the distinctive Marcan detail of the "hired servants." Gill: Christ called them "as soon as he saw them; for he was looking out for them, having appointed them long before to the service he now called them to." Ellicott marks the touch "peculiar to this Gospel, and of some interest as throwing light on the relative social position of the sons of Zebedee" — they were not the poorest of fishermen, but had a business employing hired men.

Robertson draws this out: "Zebedee and his two sons evidently had an extensive business in co-operation with Andrew and Simon (Luke 5:7, 10). Mark alone has this detail of the hired servants left with Zebedee. They left the boat and their father with the hired servants. The business would go on while they left all and became permanent followers of Jesus." He applies it tenderly to the cost borne by families: "Many a young man has faced precisely this problem when he entered the ministry. Could he leave father and mother, brothers and sisters, while he went forth... to become a fisher of men? Not the least of the sacrifices made in the education of young preachers is that made by the home folks who have additional burdens to bear."

The leaving of their father Zebedee is the costliest detail. Theophylact (under v.16) read it as a pattern: "they left their father, because he would have hindered them in following Christ. Do thou also, when thou art hindered by thy parents, leave them, and come to God" — adding that "the mother of the Apostles believed, for she followed Christ." The brothers surrendered not only their trade but the natural claims of family, leaving their father in the ship to follow the Master.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree on its force: the sons of Zebedee, though of comfortable means, "straightway" left father, ship, and business to follow Christ. The presence of the "hired servants" shows the sacrifice was real — they abandoned a going concern — yet also that the father was not left destitute. The witnesses see in their prompt obedience the same readiness shown by Simon and Andrew (v.18), and a model for all who must, at Christ's call, leave even the dearest natural ties to follow him — while Robertson reminds us of the quiet, costly share borne by the families left behind.
21:
"And they went into Capernaum; and straightway on the sabbath day he entered into the synagogue, and taught." The witnesses note the setting of Christ's first recorded Capernaum miracle and the manner of his teaching. Theophylact: on the sabbath, "when the Scribes were gathered together, He entered into a synagogue, and taught... For this end the Law commanded them to give themselves up to rest on the sabbath day, that they might meet together to attend to sacred reading." And "Christ taught them by rebuke, not by flattery as did the Pharisees." Bede contrasts him with the scribes: "The Scribes themselves taught the people what was written in Moses and the Prophets; but Jesus as the God and Lord of Moses, himself, by the freedom of His own will, either added those things which appeared wanting in the Law, or altered things... as we read, It was said to them of old time, but I say unto you."

The grammatical-historical voices supply the synagogue setting. Barnes: "the presiding elder, after reading the Scriptures, invited any who chose to address the people (Acts 13:15)... we find him often availing himself of this privilege." Robertson describes the service — "prayer, praise, reading of scripture, and exposition by any rabbi or other competent person" — and notes Capernaum (Tell Hum) was now Christ's "headquarters after the rejection in Nazareth," its synagogue affording "the best opening for the teaching of Jesus." He marks the inchoative imperfect "began to teach."

Ellicott draws attention to Mark's signature word: "The frequent recurrence of this adverb [straightway], often disguised in the English version as immediately, anon, by-and-by, should be noticed... It occurs forty-one times in the Gospel; nine times in this first chapter." Pseudo-Jerome notes Mark "follows the order of the mysteries," relating "the first miracle on the sabbath day."

The witnesses agree: Christ, having called his first disciples, enters the Capernaum synagogue on the sabbath and teaches — and the manner of his teaching (v.22) at once distinguishes him from the scribes. The sabbath gathering for sacred reading was the appointed occasion; Christ uses it, as he often would, to proclaim the kingdom. The note that he taught "as one having power" (v.22) prepares for the miracle that follows, in which the same authority that marked his words is shown to command even the unclean spirits.
22:
"And they were astonished at his doctrine: for he taught them as one that had authority, and not as the scribes." The witnesses fasten on the unique authority of Christ's teaching, which set him apart from the scribes. Gill: "they were astonished... at the manner of his preaching, which was with so much gracefulness, gravity, and majesty," unlike "the traditions of the elders" they were used to. Robertson explains the word: the people were "struck out of their senses by some strong feeling, such as fear, wonder, or even joy," and notes Mark's "as having authority" against the scribes, who "quoted other rabbis and felt their function to be expounders of the traditions which they made a millstone around the necks of the people." Here "was a personality who got his power (authority) direct from God, not from the current scribes."

Clarke names the double reason the scribes lacked authority: "because the matter of the teaching did not come from God; and because the teachers themselves were not commissioned by the Most High." Ellicott notes the verbal agreement with Matthew 7:28-29 and that "stress is laid... on the manner rather than the thing taught."

The Reformed Calvin gives the fullest theology of the contrast. The people "call it doctrine, not by way of reproach, but as an acknowledgment, that there was something in it unusual and extraordinary." Their fault lay only in remaining "in their state of hesitation." Calvin diagnoses the scribes' deadness: "As they were false expounders of Scripture, their doctrine was literal and dead, breathed nothing of the power of the Spirit, and was utterly destitute of majesty." He draws the contemporary parallel: "The same kind of coldness may now be observed in the speculative theology of Popery... what they bring forward is all affectation and mere drivelling: for the declaration of Paul holds true, that the kingdom of God is not in word but in power" (1 Corinthians 4:20). The preaching of Christ, by contrast, "was eminently distinguished by the divine power of the Spirit, which procured for him the respect of his hearers."

The witnesses agree: the astonishment of the hearers arose from the divine authority that marked Christ's teaching — original, Spirit-filled, majestic — in contrast to the lifeless, derivative exposition of the scribes. He spoke not as one citing human authorities but as one who was himself the Author of the Law. This authority in word (v.22) is immediately confirmed by authority in deed, as he commands the unclean spirit and is obeyed (vv.23-27).
23:
"And there was in their synagogue a man with an unclean spirit; and he cried out." The witnesses note the demoniac's presence in the synagogue and the encounter that confirms Christ's authority. Bede: "Since by the envy of the devil death first entered into the world, it was right that the medicine of healing should first work against the author of death... For the devils, seeing the Lord on the earth, thought that they were immediately to be judged." He reconciles Mark's "tearing him" with Luke's "hurt him not" (Luke 4:35): the "tossing of his limbs... did not weaken him."

The Latin fathers probe the term and the encounter. The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary explains why "unclean" is added — "lest by the sameness of the name [Spirit] we should fall into error" — the spirit being so called "on account of his impiousness... and because he employs himself in all unclean and wicked works." Augustine draws the great distinction: in the demons' recognition of Christ "there was knowledge, but there was not charity; and the reason was, that they feared their punishment from Him, and loved not the righteousness in Him." He was known to them "not in that He is eternal Life... but by some temporal effects of His Power." Theophylact: "to come out of man the devil considers as his own perdition; for devils are ruthless, thinking that they suffer some evil, so long as they are not troubling men."

The puritan Henry gives the fullest exposition (covering vv.23-28): "The devil is here called an unclean spirit, because he has lost all the purity of his nature... and with his suggestions he pollutes the spirits of men." The demoniac "did not come either to be taught or to be healed, but, as some think, to confront Christ." The unclean spirit "cried out, as one in an agony, at the presence of Christ... thus the devils believe and tremble, have a horror of Christ, but no hope in him."

The grammatical-historical voices note the realism. Barnes and Clarke suggest the man "had lucid intervals; else he could not have been admitted into the synagogue." Ellicott observes that "as in most Eastern cities... madness had an immunity from restraint, and the demoniacs seem to have mingled... with the crowd of worshippers." Robertson distinguishes "between the man and the unclean spirit," the demon being "in the man and the man in the unclean spirit." The witnesses agree: the first to recognize and resist Christ's authority is a demon, whose cry of agony confirms the divine power the hearers had just felt in his teaching.
24:
"Saying, Let us alone; what have we to do with thee, thou Jesus of Nazareth? art thou come to destroy us? I know thee who thou art, the Holy One of God." The witnesses unfold the demon's cry — its plea, its dread, and its forced confession. Gill: the spirit, "knowing that Christ had power to dislodge them, and fearing he would, entreats him he would let them alone." Barnes notes the plural "us": "Though but one impure spirit is mentioned... yet that spirit speaks also in the name of others. They were leagued together." The phrase "What have we to do with thee?" Clarke explains as a Hebrew idiom (as in 2 Samuel 16:10), meaning "What business hast thou with us?" — the demon protesting Christ's "improper interference," though, as Barnes adds, "the possession of the man was a direct assault on God and his works."

On "the Holy One of God," Ellicott notes the name "occurs, as applied to Christ, only here, in the parallel of Luke 4:34, and in the better MSS. of John 6:69," probably originating "in the Messianic application of Thy Holy One in Psalm 16:10." Its strict meaning is "the Holy One whom God owns as such." Barnes: the demons "had learned from his miracles that he was the Messiah, and had power over them."

The Latin fathers read the confession as knowledge without faith. The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary: the demon "calls Him holy not as one of many... but he proclaims that He was the One holy"; yet "Methinks that Thou art come" shows "he had not a firm and certain knowledge." Theophylact: the demon confesses "that they might know... from how great an evil the man was freed, and on account of the miracle might believe." Lightfoot suggests the demon exploited the saying that "in that generation... Galilee shall be laid waste," to frighten the Galileans from receiving Christ.

Henry (under v.23) summed it: the demon "calls him Jesus of Nazareth... to possess the minds of the people with low thoughts of him"; yet "a confession is extorted from him — that he is the holy One of God." Those who go no further than this notional knowledge "go no further than the devil doth." The witnesses agree: the demon's cry mingles a plea to be let alone, a dread of destruction, and a true confession wrung from him against his will — proving Christ's identity and power, yet from a source whose testimony Christ will refuse to accept (v.25).
25:
"And Jesus rebuked him, saying, Hold thy peace, and come out of him." The witnesses note Christ's refusal of the demon's testimony and the force of his command. Gill: Christ rebuked him, "despising his flattery, and refusing to receive a testimony from him; and which he wanted not, lest it should be thought he had a familiarity and confederacy with him." Barnes draws the doctrinal point: "This was not the man that he rebuked, but the spirit... His conversation was with evil spirit — proving conclusively that it was not a mere disease, or derangement — for how could the Son of God hold converse with disease?... but that he conversed with a being, who also conversed, reasoned, cavilled, felt, resisted, and knew him."

The command "Hold thy peace" the grammatical voices render vividly. Ellicott: "Literally, be still, be gagged. The same verb is used in the calming of the winds and waves in Mark 4:39." Robertson: "Be muzzled like an ox," as in Deuteronomy 25:4 and 1 Corinthians 9:9 — Gould rendering it "Shut up." Clarke notes the kind of spirit that "will only yield to the sovereign power of the Son of God."

The Reformed Calvin (under v.22) had explained why Christ silenced the demon: "the devil dexterously acknowledges that Christ is [the Holy One], in order to insinuate into the minds of men a suspicion, that there was some secret understanding between him and Christ." Christ "rebukes him" lest his glory be soiled by such testimony; for "Satan cannot endure that power... he would desire that Christ should satisfy himself with an empty title, without exercising it." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary (under v.23) drew the precept: "let us not believe devils, howsoever they may proclaim the truth."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, the turning-point of the exorcism. The witnesses agree: Christ silences and expels the demon by a word, refusing the testimony of an unclean spirit and displaying the very authority the hearers had marvelled at in his teaching (v.22). He will not have his name honoured by the father of lies, nor allow any pretext for the charge of collusion. The single word of command — to be silent and to come out — is enough; the spirit that had cried out and resisted must obey. Henry (under v.23) caught the lesson: "Christ has a muzzle for that unclean spirit when he fawns as well as when he barks," disdaining a confession made "under the cloak of that profession" to carry on "malicious mischievous designs."
26:
"And when the unclean spirit had torn him, and cried with a loud voice, he came out of him." The witnesses note the violence of the demon's departure and reconcile Mark's "torn" with Luke's "hurt him not." Gill clarifies: "not that he had torn any limb from him, or had made any wound... but he shook him; and threw him in the midst," convulsing him. Robertson: "Margin, convulsing him like a spasm," with "the loud voice, a screech, in fact. It was a moment of intense excitement."

Barnes draws the moral from the demon's last act of malice: "Still malignant, though doomed to obey — submitting because he was obliged to, not because he chose — he exerted his last power, inflicted all the pain he could, and then bowed to the Son of God, and came out. This is the nature of an evil disposition. Though compelled to obey... it does all the ill it can." Clarke observes that "never was there a person possessed by an unclean spirit who did not suffer a convulsion," and reads the violent throes as "the last efforts of the impure spirit, who has great rages because he knows his time is but short."

The Reformed Calvin harmonizes the accounts: Mark and Luke "agree perfectly... the devil went out of the man in a violent manner. He threw down the unhappy man, as if he had intended to tear him: but Luke says that the attempt was unsuccessful" — not that there was no injury or pain at all, "but that the man was afterwards delivered from the devil, and restored to perfect health." Wesley notes the loud cry was uttered though the demon "was forbidden to speak," and that "Christ would neither suffer those evil spirits to speak in opposition, nor yet in favour of him."

Henry (under v.23) had drawn the application to the soul's deliverance: "when Christ by his grace delivers poor souls out of the hands of Satan, it is not without a grievous toss and tumult in the soul; for that spiteful enemy will disquiet those whom he cannot destroy." The witnesses agree: the demon, forced to obey Christ's word, vents his impotent rage in a final convulsion and screech, yet does the man no lasting harm. The violent departure both displays the reality and malice of the evil spirit and magnifies the power of Christ, whose single command compels the enemy to leave — a picture, for the witnesses, of every gracious deliverance from Satan's power, which the enemy resists to the last but cannot prevent.
27:
"And they were all amazed, insomuch that they questioned among themselves, saying, What thing is this? what new doctrine is this? for with authority commandeth he even the unclean spirits, and they do obey him." The witnesses note the people's astonishment, which linked the authority of Christ's teaching to the authority of his deed. Barnes: "The power of casting out devils was to them new. It was done by a word. He did it in his own name, and by his own authority. This proved that he was superior to all the unclean spirits." Robertson catches the freshness: "The teaching was fresh, original as the dew of the morning on the blossoms just blown. That was a novelty in that synagogue where only staid and stilted rabbinical rules had been heretofore droned out." He notes the most astonishing thing was that the demons "obey him" — for the people "were accustomed to the use of magical formulae by the Jewish exorcists (Acts 19:13), but here was something utterly different."

Ellicott notes a textual variant giving a different structure: "What thing is this? A new doctrine with power. He commandeth even the unclean spirits..." — the word "doctrine" meaning, as in v.22, "the teaching taken as a whole, including manner as well as substance." Clarke marks the people's reasoning: "they had never heard such a gracious doctrine, and never saw any teaching supported by miracles before. How much must this person be superior to men!"

The Reformed Calvin guards the people's word: "They call it doctrine, not by way of reproach, but as an acknowledgment, that there was something in it unusual and extraordinary... This is rather a part of their admiration." Their only fault is "that they remain in their state of hesitation, whereas the children of God ought to make increasing progress."

The witnesses agree: the miracle confirmed the teaching. The same authority the hearers had marvelled at in Christ's words (v.22) was now displayed in his command over the unclean spirits, and the two together pressed upon them the question of his identity. The deed authenticated the doctrine: here was no mere expounder of traditions, but One whose word the very demons obeyed. Yet the witnesses note the danger of stopping at amazement; the crowd questioned and wondered, but (Calvin) ought to have advanced from astonishment to faith. The miracle was meant not merely to startle but to lead to belief in the One whose authority it displayed.
28:
"And immediately his fame spread abroad throughout all the region round about Galilee." The brief verse records the rapid spread of Christ's reputation after the synagogue miracle. Gill notes his fame spread "not only in the city of Capernaum, where these things were done... but also" through the surrounding region. Robertson: "They had no telephones, telegraphs, newspapers or radio, but news has a marvellous way of spreading by word of mouth. The fame of this new teacher went out everywhere throughout all Galilee."

Clarke analyzes why the report spread so fast: the miracle was "great"; it "evidenced much benevolence in the worker of it"; and it "was very public, being wrought in the synagogue. The many who saw it published it wherever they went; and thus the fame of Christ, as an incomparable teacher, and unparalleled worker of miracles, became soon spread abroad through the land." He also remarks on Mark's characteristic style: the word "immediately" (eutheōs) "occurs more frequently in this evangelist than in any other writer of the new covenant," used "as our ancient writers used forsooth."

Henry (under v.23) had drawn out the effect: "Immediately his fame spread abroad into the whole adjacent region of Galilee... it was universally concluded that he was a Teacher come from God." The miracle, wrought publicly in the synagogue and confirming his authoritative teaching, naturally drew the attention of the whole countryside, and prepared the crowds who would soon flock to him (vv.32-33).

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which marks the transition from the synagogue miracle to the wider Galilean ministry. The witnesses agree on its simple force: the report of Christ's authority — in teaching and in casting out the demon — spread swiftly through all Galilee by word of mouth. The publicity of the miracle, done in the synagogue before the assembled worshippers, ensured that his fame could not be contained; and the spreading report both magnified his ministry and gathered the multitudes who would press upon him for healing. Mark's repeated "immediately" gives the narrative its breathless pace, fitting for a Gospel that, as the witnesses have noted, is "emphatically the Evangelist of action" (Ellicott). The fame that spread, however, was as yet only wonder at his power; the deeper question of who he was, and the call to faith, still lay before the crowds who heard the report.
29:
"And forthwith, when they were come out of the synagogue, they entered into the house of Simon and Andrew, with James and John." The witnesses note the second Capernaum miracle — the healing of Peter's mother-in-law — and its domestic, eyewitness quality. Robertson, hearing Peter behind Mark: "Peter was married and both he and Andrew lived together in Peter's house with Peter's wife and mother-in-law... Mark heard Peter tell it as it occurred in his own house where Jesus made his home while in Capernaum." Mark's vivid detail — "lay sick of a fever" (lay prostrate burning) — and the "touch of Jesus on her hand" make "a tender scene." Peter was "evidently married before he began to follow Jesus" (1 Corinthians 9:5).

The puritan Henry draws the lessons (covering vv.29-39): after the synagogue, Christ "did not sit still... he continued to do good"; he "conversed familiarly with the poor fishermen," accepting the hospitality of Peter's house. On the cure: "Wherever Christ comes, he comes to do good, and will be sure to pay richly for his entertainment." And it was complete: "when the fever left her, it did not, as usual, leave her weak, but the same hand that healed her, strengthened her, so that she was able to minister to them; the cure is in order to that, to fit for action, that we may minister to Christ."

The Latin fathers read it morally. Bede: it was fitting that "the woman, who was first seduced, should be healed from the fever of carnal concupiscence," and the instant restored strength shows "the health which is conferred at the command of the Lord, returns at once entire." Pseudo-Jerome: "the fever means intemperance, from which we... by the hand of discipline... are healed, and minister to the will of Him who heals us."

The Reformed Calvin notes that "Matthew does not relate this history in its proper order," and that the Evangelists "took particular notice of this miracle" because by it "Christ gave to his disciples a private and familiar illustration of his grace," and because "the healing of one woman gave occasion to many miracles." He marks the "clearer and more affecting proof of divine power, that, in a moment, and by a single touch, he removed a strong and violent disease." The witnesses agree: in the intimate setting of Peter's home, Christ heals his host's mother-in-law instantly and completely, so that she rises to serve — a tender, eyewitness scene that displays both his power and his readiness to do good in private as in public.
30:
"But Simon's wife's mother lay sick of a fever, and anon they tell him of her." The brief verse — with only Gill's comment in the Catena — sets the scene of the second Capernaum miracle. Gill notes that Luke calls it "a great fever," "a very violent one, which threatened with death, and must be very dangerous to an old person."

The detail that the disciples "tell him of her" — or, as Luke has it, "besought him for her" — shows the immediate recourse of the household to Christ in their distress. Bede (under v.29) had drawn the lesson that "the Saviour sometimes after being asked, sometimes of His own accord, heals the sick, shewing that He always assents to the prayers of the faithful." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary (under v.29) added that "the disciples, knowing that they were to receive a benefit by that means, without waiting for the evening prayed that Peter's mother should be healed."

The severity of the fever — "great," violent, life-threatening to an aged woman — magnifies the cure that follows. This was no slight ailment that might pass of itself, but a dangerous disease; and its instant and complete removal by Christ's touch (v.31) displays his power the more strikingly. The household's prompt appeal to Christ on her behalf models the recourse of faith: when sickness strikes those dear to us, we are to bring them at once to the Lord.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which is purely transitional, introducing the sufferer and the appeal. The witnesses (here only Gill) add little beyond noting the gravity of the fever, drawing on Luke's fuller "great fever" and the Jewish sense of its danger to the elderly. The verse prepares for the immediate cure of v.31, where Christ takes her by the hand and lifts her up, the fever leaving her at once. Taken with the surrounding context, it forms part of the eyewitness picture — Peter's own house, his own mother-in-law, the disciples' anxious appeal — that gives this brief Capernaum scene its tender, domestic quality, and that displays Christ's grace at work not only in the public synagogue but in the private home of his disciple. The fervent appeal of the household, and Christ's ready response, together teach that no concern of his people is too small or too domestic for his notice.
31:
"And he came and took her by the hand, and lifted her up; and immediately the fever left her, and she ministered unto them." The brief verse — with only Gill in the Catena — records the manner and the result of the cure. Gill notes Christ "went into the room where she lay, and took hold of her hand; not to feel her pulse, and thereby judge of the nature and strength of her disorder, as physicians do; nor merely in a friendly manner, as is customary, but in order to restore her."

The gesture of taking her by the hand and lifting her up the witnesses (drawing on the parallel passages) read as both the means and the sign of the cure. Henry (under v.29) had noted the completeness: "the same hand that healed her, strengthened her, so that she was able to minister to them." Calvin (under v.29) marked that Christ "either to mark his affection, or because he was aware that this sign was at that time advantageous," used the outward touch, though "he might have done it by the slightest expression of his will." The fever left her "immediately" — Mark's characteristic word — and so completely that, contrary to the usual weakness that follows a violent fever, she rose at once to serve.

The detail "she ministered unto them" is the witnesses' favourite note. The cure was "in order to that, to fit for action, that we may minister to Christ, and to those that are his for his sake" (Henry). Theophylact (under v.29) had drawn the moral: "God will heal a sick man, if he ministers to the Saints, through love to Christ." The restored woman's first act was service — a model of the proper response to Christ's grace, which heals us not for idleness but for ministry.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree on its twin lessons: the completeness of Christ's cure, which not only removes the disease but restores full strength; and the purpose of healing, which is service. The hand of Christ, taking hers and lifting her up, both healed and strengthened; and she who had been bedridden with a dangerous fever rose at once to wait upon her guests. The instant, total recovery — without the convalescence that follows ordinary fevers — displays the divine power of the Healer; and her immediate ministry displays the fitting fruit of his grace. So, the witnesses imply, all whom Christ heals and raises are raised to serve him and his people. Grace received issues in grace returned, and the strength Christ gives is given for use.
32:
"And at even, when the sun did set, they brought unto him all that were diseased, and them that were possessed with devils." The witnesses note the timing — the close of the sabbath — and the great evening throng. Theophylact: "Because the multitude thought that it was not lawful to heal on the sabbath day, they waited for the evening, to bring those who were to be healed to Jesus." Gill confirms: "at which time the Jews' sabbath was ended... the sabbath was over; and therefore being under no restraint on account of that," they brought their sick. Robertson: "The sabbath ended at sunset and so the people were now at liberty to bring their sick to Jesus."

The grammatical voices mark Mark's vivid detail. Robertson: "This picturesque detail Mark has besides at even... They brought them in a steady stream (imperfect tense). Luke adds that Jesus laid his hand on every one of them as they passed by in grateful procession." Ellicott notes Mark's fuller description (v.33) that "all the city was gathered together at the door."

On "many" (v.34), Theophylact and the Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary explain it as a Scriptural way of saying "all": Theophylact, "He healed many of those who were brought, that is, all who had faith"; Pseudo-Chrysostom, "all are to be understood according to the Scripture mode of expression." The Pseudo-Augustine note adds why Christ forbade the demons to speak: "to teach us not to believe them, even if they say true. For if once they find persons to believe them, they mingle truth with falsehood."

The Latin fathers read the setting sun spiritually. Bede: "the setting of the sun signifies the passion of Him, who said, As long as I am in the world, I am the light of the world (John 9:5). And when the sun was going down, more demoniacs and sick persons were healed than before: because He who living in the flesh... taught a few Jews, has transmitted the gifts of faith and health to all the Gentiles."

The witnesses agree: as the sabbath closed at sunset, the whole city brought their sick and demon-possessed to Christ, who healed them in a steady stream. The crowd's waiting until evening, out of scruple over the sabbath, shows both their reverence for the law and their eagerness for Christ's healing; and the great throng at Peter's door (v.33) displays the effect of the synagogue miracle, which had spread his fame through all Galilee (v.28). The setting sun, for the fathers, foreshadows the wider grace to come to the Gentiles.
33:
"And all the city was gathered together at the door." The brief verse — with Gill, Barnes, Robertson, and Wesley — captures the vividness of the scene at Peter's house. Gill: "the inhabitants of the city of Capernaum, a very great number of them at least, who having either heard of, or seen the dispossession of the unclean spirit in the synagogue... got together in great crowds about the door of the house of Simon and Andrew where Jesus now was." Barnes takes "all the city" as "a great part of the city. A great multitude from the city."

Robertson hears Peter's eyewitness memory behind the detail: "The whole city was gathered together there (past perfect passive periphrastic indicative, double compound). Mark alone mentions this vivid detail. He is seeing with Peter's eyes again. Peter no doubt watched the beautiful scene with pride and gratitude as Jesus stood in the door and healed the great crowds in the glory of that sunset. He loved to tell it afterwards."

The picture of the whole city pressed about the single door, with Christ standing in the doorway healing the multitudes in the light of the setting sun, is one of the Gospel's most vivid scenes — and, the witnesses note, distinctively Mark's, drawn from Peter's recollection. The throng at the door measures the impact of the day's miracles, which had spread Christ's fame through all the region (v.28); now the sick and the possessed of the whole town are brought to him as soon as the sabbath allows.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree on its force: the gathering of the entire city at Peter's door displays both the desperate need of the people and the magnetic power of Christ's compassion. Wesley adds a wistful note on the fruitlessness that would follow such promise: "O what a fair prospect was here! Who could then have imagined that all these blossoms would die away without fruit?" — for many who flocked for healing would not, in the end, believe. The scene thus stands as both a display of Christ's grace freely poured out and a foreshadowing of the tragedy of unbelief: a whole city at his door, yet few who would truly follow. The crowds came for the body's cure; the deeper healing of the soul, which only faith receives, most of them would never seek.
34:
"And he healed many that were sick of divers diseases, and cast out many devils; and suffered not the devils to speak, because they knew him." The witnesses address the comprehensiveness of the healing and the silencing of the demons. Gill guards against misreading "many": "Not that there were some... whom he did not heal; but he healed all that came, or were brought to him, which were many, of every sort of disease." (Theophylact and Pseudo-Chrysostom, under v.32, had likewise taken "many" as "all who had faith," a Scriptural idiom.)

On the silencing of the demons, the grammatical voices note the reason. Barnes: "They knew that he was the Messiah. If they had spoken, they would have made that known to the people. Jesus was not desirous, at that time, that that should be publicly known." Ellicott cites Luke 4:41: "The demoniacs had cried out, Thou art the Son of God. They knew that He was the Christ." Robertson: "Would not allow, imperfect tense of continued refusal... a direct reference to 1:24 when in the synagogue the demon recognized and addressed Jesus as the Holy One of God.
