Testimony from such a source was not calculated to help the cause of Christ with the people."

The Reformed Calvin gives two reasons for the silencing: "a general reason, because the time of the full revelation was not yet come; and a special reason... that he refused to have, as heralds and witnesses of his divinity, those whose praise could have no other effect than to soil and injure his character. For he must have known, that the prince of death... [is] in a state of irreconcileable enmity with the Author of eternal salvation and life." Wesley pointedly rejects the rationalist reading: "according to Dr. Mead's hypothesis (that the Scriptural demoniacs were only diseased persons), He suffered not the diseases to say that they knew him!" — exposing the absurdity of denying real demonic possession.

The witnesses agree: Christ healed all who were brought, of every kind of disease, and cast out many demons — but refused to let the demons publish his identity. The two reasons converge: the time for the full revelation of his Messiahship had not come, and the testimony of unclean spirits could only soil his honour. His power was thus displayed in both the healing of bodies and the mastery of demons, while his wisdom appeared in refusing the praise of his enemies. The witnesses see here, against the rationalists, clear proof of real demonic agency — for Christ does not silence diseases, but beings who "knew him."
35:
"And in the morning, rising up a great while before day, he went out, and departed into a solitary place, and there prayed." The witnesses dwell on Christ's habit of early, solitary prayer and draw the lesson for believers. The Latin and Antiochene fathers note the example. Theophylact: "By which He taught us not to do any thing for the sake of appearance... He shews to us that we ought to attribute to God whatever we do well." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary guards the truth: "Not that He required prayer; for it was He who Himself received the prayers of men; but He did this by way of an economy, and became to us the model of good work."

The grammatical-historical Barnes makes the verse a meditation on morning prayer: Christ, "though perfectly holy, regarded the duty of secret prayer as of great importance"; "he sought a solitary place... far away from the world"; "it was early in the morning — the first thing after rising — always the best time." He draws the rule: "the religious enjoyment through the day will be according to the state of the heart in the morning... If that is omitted, all will go wrong. Our piety will wither." Robertson reconciles Mark's "a great while before day" with Luke's "when it was day": "in the early part of the watch while it was still a bit dark," picturing Jesus "praying through the early morning hours." He notes the retreat "before his sudden popularity, to prayer with the Father that he might not be ensnared by this popularity."

The puritan Henry (under v.29) had drawn it out fully: "Though as God he was prayed to, as man he prayed... Though he was glorifying God, and doing good, in his public work, yet he found time to be alone with his Father." The time was "the morning after the sabbath day... we must not think that we may intermit our devotion till the next sabbath." The place was "a solitary place... to set us an example to his own rule, When thou prayest enter into thy closet." Clarke: "Every thing that our blessed Lord did he performed either as our pattern, or as our sacrifice."

The witnesses agree: Christ, after a day of crowded labour and on the crest of popularity, rose before dawn and withdrew to a solitary place to pray. His example teaches the importance of early, secret, solitary prayer — a discipline he, though sinless and very God, did not neglect, and which he leaves as a pattern for all his servants. The retreat also guarded him from being captured by the crowds' enthusiasm, keeping his ministry directed by the Father's will rather than the people's demands (vv.37-38).
36:
"And Simon and they that were with him followed after him." The brief verse records the disciples' pursuit of the praying Christ. Gill identifies them: "Peter, and his brother Andrew, together with James and John." Robertson catches the force of the verb: "Hunted him out (Moffatt). Perfective use of the preposition kata (down to the finish). The verb is used for the hunt or chase, pursuit... Peter and those with him kept up the search till they found him." He hears Peter's own story: "Simon's intention at least was good; the Master seemed to be losing precious opportunities and must be brought back" (Swete).

Clarke reads the pursuit as zeal for Christ's word: "katediōxan, followed him eagerly. They had now begun to taste the good word of God, and thought they could never hear too much of it. Many possess this spirit when first converted to God. O! what a pity that they should ever lose it!" Ellicott notes that Luke alone gives their motive — "they stayed Him that He should not depart from them" — and that the definite statement of who followed is "peculiar to St. Mark."

The disciples' eager pursuit, "hunting him out" to his place of prayer, sprang from a good but imperfect motive. They were proud of his popularity and eager that he should return to the waiting crowds (v.37). Yet, as the witnesses note, their zeal mistook the Master's mind: Christ had withdrawn precisely to escape the pressure of the crowds and to seek the Father's direction, and he would not be governed by the people's demands or the disciples' enthusiasm (v.38).

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which sets up the exchange of vv.37-38. The witnesses agree on its sense: the disciples, finding their Master gone in the early morning, eagerly searched him out and pursued him to his solitary place of prayer. Their pursuit was well-meant — born of a fresh-converted hunger for his word and a pride in his growing fame — but it betrayed a misunderstanding of his purpose. They would have kept him at Capernaum to enjoy his ministry; he had a wider mission in view. The verse thus prepares for Christ's gentle correction of their narrow zeal, as he turns from the popularity they prized to the preaching tour the Father had appointed.
37:
"And when they had found him, they said unto him, All men seek for thee." The brief verse records the disciples' report to the praying Christ. Gill notes they found him "in the desert and solitary place, where he had been praying." Barnes explains the report's intent: "The inquiry after him was general... They told him this evidently with a view to induce him to leave his place of retirement, and to prevail upon him to appear publicly, to instruct the multitudes."

The words "All men seek for thee" express both the disciples' pride in his popularity and their wish that he return to Capernaum. Henry (under v.29) had drawn it out: "They were proud that their Master was become so popular already, and would have him appear in public, yet more in that place, because it was their own city; and we are apt to be partial to the places we know and are interested in." The disciples, finding the whole city again seeking him, supposed he should hasten back to the eager crowds.

Clarke reflects on the mixed motives of those who sought him: "Some to hear; some to be healed; some to be saved; and some, perhaps, through no good motive. There are all sorts of followers in the train of Christ; but how few walk steadily, and persevere unto the end!" The general seeking after Christ was not all of one kind; and the popularity the disciples prized was, in part, the shallow enthusiasm of crowds drawn by his miracles rather than by faith.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which forms the disciples' half of the exchange completed in v.38. The witnesses agree on its force: the disciples, having found Christ at prayer, urge him to return because "all men seek for thee" — a report meant to draw him back to the Capernaum crowds. But Christ, who had withdrawn to seek the Father's will, would not be turned by popularity. His answer (v.38) redirects their narrow zeal to the wider mission: he must preach in the other towns also, "for therefore came I forth." The verse thus exposes the gap between the disciples' wish — to keep the Master where the crowds were greatest — and Christ's purpose, to carry the gospel throughout Galilee. The pull of present popularity, the witnesses imply, must yield to the call of the larger work.
38:
"And he said unto them, Let us go into the next towns, that I may preach there also: for therefore came I forth." The witnesses note Christ's refusal to be confined by popularity and his sense of a wider mission. Gill explains the word for "towns" — "village cities," places "neither villages, nor cities, but between both," having synagogues. Lightfoot, with rabbinic learning, distinguishes walled cities, towns, and villages. Ellicott: the word "occurs here only... seems to have been coined to express the character of such places as Bethsaida, Chorazin... which were more than villages, yet could hardly be classed as cities."

On "that I may preach there also," the witnesses stress the priority of preaching. Ellicott: Luke gives more fully "to publish the good news of the kingdom of God," the word "preach" having "its full significance of proclaiming, doing a herald's office." Barnes: "Since multitudes press to hear the word, let us not remain here, but go into the neighbouring towns also." Clarke draws the ministerial lesson: "The towns and the villages will not come to the preacher — the preacher must go to them, if he desires their salvation... Let no minister of God think he has delivered his own soul, till he has made an offer of salvation to every city and village within his reach."

On "for therefore came I forth," the witnesses weigh the reference. Barnes and Ellicott note Luke's parallel, "for therefore am I sent" (compare John 16:28, "I came forth from the Father"). Ellicott: in this form the words "might refer simply to His leaving Capernaum," but Luke's "sent" "connects them with His mission as a whole" — though the disciples would as yet understand only his coming forth from Nazareth, not "from the bosom of the Father." Robertson notes "it was a surprising decision for Jesus to leave the eager, excited throngs in Capernaum for the country town."

The Reformed Calvin sees in the words "a declaration of his earnest desire to fulfil his office": Christ's design "was agreeable to the injunction and call of the Father," for "it was necessary that Christ should travel, within a short period, throughout Judea, to awaken the minds of men... as if by the sound of a trumpet, to hear the Gospel." The witnesses agree: Christ, declining to remain where his popularity was greatest, presses on to the surrounding towns to preach — for proclaiming the kingdom, not gathering crowds in one place, was the purpose for which he came forth.
39:
"And he preached in their synagogues throughout all Galilee, and cast out devils." The brief verse summarizes Christ's Galilean preaching tour, joining word and deed. Gill: he "preached in their synagogues, which were in the next towns, in the village cities." Barnes cross-references the parallel summaries (Matthew 4:23). The verse pairs preaching with the casting out of demons, as Christ's doctrine and his power over Satan went together (as in the Capernaum synagogue, vv.21-27).

Clarke draws out the perseverance: the imperfect verb means "he continued preaching — he never slackened his pace — he continued proclaiming the glad tidings of salvation to all — there was no time to be lost — immortal souls were perishing for lack of knowledge; and the grand adversary was prowling about, seeking whom he might devour. This zealous, affectionate, and persevering diligence of Christ should be copied by all his servants in the ministry." His prayer: "O God, send forth more and more faithful laborers into thy vineyard!"

Henry (under v.29) had drawn the connection of preaching and exorcism: "to illustrate and confirm his doctrine, he cast out devils. Note, Christ's doctrine is Satan's destruction. Wherever Christ comes, he comes to do good." The two go together: the preaching of the kingdom is itself the overthrow of the devil's kingdom, and the visible casting out of demons confirms and illustrates the spiritual deliverance the gospel brings.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which closes the account of Christ's first Galilean circuit. The witnesses agree on its force: faithful to the purpose he had just declared (v.38), Christ went throughout all Galilee, preaching in the synagogues and casting out demons. His ministry was one of unwearied diligence — continuous proclamation joined to the destruction of Satan's works. The summary displays both the breadth of his early ministry (all Galilee, town by town) and its character (word confirmed by power), and it leaves a pattern for his servants: persevering, zealous proclamation of the gospel, undertaken in the urgent knowledge that souls are perishing and the adversary is at work. The casting out of devils, set beside the preaching, declares that the gospel Christ proclaimed was no mere teaching but the inbreaking of God's kingdom against the powers of darkness.
40:
"And there came a leper to him, beseeching him, and kneeling down to him, and saying unto him, If thou wilt, thou canst make me clean." The witnesses dwell on the leper's faith and humility, and Christ's compassionate, authoritative cure. The Latin fathers read it richly. Bede: the leper, "excluded by the Law, inferring that he was cleansed by the power of the Lord, shewed that grace... was not from the Law, but over the Law." His posture and words show both faith and reverence: "He falls on his face, which is at once a gesture of lowliness and of shame... but his shame did not stifle confession; he shewed his wound, and begged for medicine," referring "the power to the will of the Lord." His doubt was "not as disbelieving His compassion, but, as conscious of his own filth, he did not presume."

The puritan Henry teaches "how to apply ourselves to Christ": with humility ("kneeling down"), with "a firm belief of his power" ("Thou canst make me clean"), and with "submission to the will of Christ" ("Lord, if thou wilt"). "What we believe of the power of Christ we must bring home to our particular case." Christ's answer matches the request: "I will be confident of Christ's power... and Christ will show how much his power is drawn out into act by the faith of his people."

The fathers explain why Christ touched the leper, though the Law called such touch defiling. Chrysostom: "to shew that He Himself is the Lord of the Law... He gives health, not as a servant, but as the Lord"; the touch "proved that He could not be defiled, who frees others from pollution" (Bede). Bede notes Christ "healed in the way in which He had been begged to heal": "If Thou wilt, Thou canst" — "I will, be clean."

The grammatical-historical Barnes: the leper kneeled "in token of deep humiliation, and earnest entreaty"; "the fact that Jesus touched him was evidence that the requisite power had been already put forth"; and "None but God can work a miracle. Yet Jesus does it by his own will... He was, therefore, Divine." Bede explains the charge to silence and the sending to the priest: that "the priest might understand that the leper was not healed by the Law, but by the grace of God above the Law." The witnesses agree: the leper's faith ("thou canst") joined to his submission ("if thou wilt") drew from Christ both compassion and the sovereign word, "I will; be thou clean" — a cure that displays Christ as Lord over the Law and over the disease.
41:
"And Jesus, moved with compassion, put forth his hand, and touched him, and saith unto him, I will; be thou clean." The brief verse — with Gill and Robertson in the Catena — fixes on the compassion that moved Christ to heal. Gill: Christ was moved with compassion "at the sad and deplorable case the poor man was in, being a merciful high priest, and not with a desire of popular applause, and vain glory." Robertson notes the word is "only in Mark" — splanchnistheis, "being moved with compassion," from the verb for the inward parts, the seat of deep feeling.

This detail of Christ's compassion is Mark's distinctive addition. The cleansing of the leper was not a cold display of power but the outflow of genuine pity for a man in a wretched and excluded condition. Henry (under v.40) had drawn it out: "Christ's power is employed by his pity for the relief of poor souls; that his reasons are fetched from within himself, and we have nothing in us to recommend us to his favour, but our misery makes us the objects of his mercy. And what he does for us he does with all possible tenderness."

The three acts of the verse — the inward compassion, the outstretched hand and touch, the authoritative word — together display the manner of Christ's healing. He was moved within (compassion); he reached out (the touch, which by the Law would defile, yet which showed he could not be defiled but rather cleansed); and he spoke with sovereign authority ("I will; be thou clean"), answering the leper's "if thou wilt, thou canst" (v.40) with the matching "I will." Bede (under v.40) marked the immediacy: "there is no interval between the work of God and the command, because the work is in the command, for He commanded, and they were created" (Psalm 148:5).

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree on its central note: it was compassion, not the desire for applause, that moved Christ to cleanse the leper. Mark alone records this inward motive, giving us a glimpse of the tender heart of the Healer. The touch and the word that follow are the outward expression of that compassion — the hand that defiles none but cleanses, and the voice whose command is itself the cure. The verse thus reveals not only Christ's power but his pity: he heals because he is moved with compassion, and our very misery is the plea that draws his mercy. The leper, excluded and unclean, found in Christ a high priest "moved with compassion" who reached out to touch the untouchable.
42:
"And as soon as he had spoken, immediately the leprosy departed from him, and he was cleansed." The briefest verse of the chapter — with only Gill's opening words in the Catena — records the instant and complete cure. The cleansing followed Christ's word "as soon as he had spoken": there was no interval, no process, no gradual recovery, but an immediate and total departure of the leprosy at the command "Be thou clean" (v.41).

Bede (under v.40) had drawn out this immediacy: "there is no interval between the work of God and the command, because the work is in the command, for He commanded, and they were created" (Psalm 148:5). The word of Christ is itself the deed; to command and to accomplish are, for him, one. The leprosy — that loathsome, incurable, defiling disease, in the Law a figure of sin — vanished at once and entirely, leaving the man clean.

Henry (under v.40) marked the same: "power accompanied this word, and the cure was perfect in an instant; Immediately his leprosy vanished, and there remained no more sign of it." The completeness of the cure (no remaining trace) and its instantaneousness (at the very moment of the word) together display the divine power of Christ. No physician's art could so cleanse a leper; the cure was the act of One who, as Barnes said (under v.40), "does it by his own will... and was therefore Divine."

The thin comment matches the shortest verse in the chapter. The witnesses, having said all that is needed under vv.40-41, let the bare fact stand: the leprosy departed immediately at Christ's word, and the man was cleansed. The verse is the hinge between the cure (vv.40-41) and the charge that follows (vv.43-44): Christ, having cleansed the leper by a word, strictly charges him to tell no one but to show himself to the priest. The instant, complete cleansing — the leprosy gone the moment Christ spoke — stands as one of the Gospel's clearest demonstrations that the word of Christ carries the power of God, and that his compassion (v.41) issues not in mere sympathy but in real and total deliverance. As leprosy figured sin, so this cleansing figures the cleansing of the soul: at the word of Christ, the defilement departs, and the unclean is made clean.
43:
"And he straitly charged him, and forthwith sent him away." The witnesses note the force of the stern charge Christ laid on the cleansed leper. Robertson explains the word, "only in Mark": embrimēsamenos, "strictly charged" — "a strong word for the snorting of a horse [that] expresses powerful emotion as Jesus stood here face to face with leprosy, itself a symbol of sin and all its train of evils." Ellicott notes it is "the same as that in Matthew 9:30." The charge had two parts (unfolded in v.44): the command to report to the priest, "in accord with the Mosaic regulations," and "the prohibition against talking about it... to allay excitement and to avoid needless opposition to Christ" (Robertson).

Gill takes the stern charge in a moral direction: it was given "either with the sin which had been the cause of this leprosy, and to take care that he sinned that sin no more, lest a worse evil should befall him; for sin was usually the cause of leprosy, as the cases of Miriam, Gehazi, and Uzziah show." He recounts the rabbinic tradition that leprosy came "for seven things," the "seven abominations" of Proverbs 6 — a proud look, a lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood, and the rest — illustrating each from Scripture.

Henry (under v.40) had taken the "straitly charged" similarly, as more than the charge to silence: "I am apt to think that this refers... to such a charge as he gave to the impotent man whom he cured (John 5:14), Sin no more, lest a worse thing come unto thee; for the leprosy was ordinarily the punishment of some particular sinners... now, when Christ healed him, he warned him... of the fatal consequence of it if he should return to sin."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree on the intensity of the charge — Mark's strong word marking Christ's powerful emotion before the symbol of sin — and on its double purpose: to send the man to the priest in obedience to the Law, and to forbid him to publish the cure. Some (Gill, Henry) hear in the stern charge also a warning against returning to the sin that may have occasioned the leprosy. The verse thus joins the cure (vv.40-42) to the instructions (v.44): Christ, having cleansed the leper, does not leave him to his own devices but charges him solemnly — to obey the Law, to avoid needless tumult, and (perhaps) to sin no more. Grace received carries with it command to be obeyed.
44:
"And saith unto him, See thou say nothing to any man: but go thy way, shew thyself to the priest, and offer for thy cleansing those things which Moses commanded, for a testimony unto them." The witnesses unfold the double command — silence and obedience to the Law — and the meaning of "a testimony." On the charge to silence, the fathers (under v.40) explained it as Christ's humility and avoidance of tumult; Chrysostom: "It is not yet time that My works should be preached, I require not thy preaching... not to seek worldly honour as a reward for our works."

On the sending to the priest, Chrysostom: "Our Saviour sent him to the priest for the trial of his cure, and that he might not be cast out of the temple... He sends him also, that he might fulfil all the parts of the Law, in order to stop the evil-speaking tongue of the Jews." Bede (under v.40): "This He did in order that the priest might understand that the leper was not healed by the Law, but by the grace of God above the Law"; and the Jewish sacrifice was still ordered because Christ "had not yet offered His own holocaust in His passion."

On "for a testimony unto them," the Reformed Calvin argues the pronoun refers "to the priests," not (as some) to a statute: the miracle "was afterwards to be a sufficiently clear proof for convicting them of ingratitude," and the leper's coming to the priest at Christ's command was "that which would render them inexcusable, if they refused to receive Christ... and would take away occasion for slander, since Christ did not neglect a single point of the law." Robertson agrees: "Without the formal testimony of the priests the people would not receive the leper as officially clean." Wesley draws out the legal force: the priest's pronouncing him clean "was such a proof against them, that they durst never say the leper was not cleansed" — and this, he adds, is "one great reason why our Lord commanded this man to say nothing," lest report reach the priests before the man arrived.

Wesley also marks the difference for us: "But our blessed Lord gives no such charge to us. If he has made us clean from our leprosy of sin, we are not commanded to conceal it. On the contrary, it is our duty to publish it abroad." The witnesses agree: Christ commanded the cleansed leper to be silent and to fulfil the Mosaic law by showing himself to the priest and offering the prescribed sacrifices — both to authenticate the cure officially and to leave the priests a testimony that would convict them if they rejected the One who, while honouring every point of the Law, had healed by a grace above it.
45:
"But he went out, and began to publish it much, and to blaze abroad the matter, insomuch that Jesus could no more openly enter into the city, but was without in desert places: and they came to him from every quarter." The witnesses note the leper's disobedient publicity and its effect on Christ's movements. Barnes: the man "was so deeply affected with [his cure], and so much rejoiced, that he followed the natural dictates of his own feelings, rather than the command of the Saviour." The result: "Jesus could no more openly enter into the city" — not from "any natural inability... but only denotes that there was difficulty, or inconvenience, or impropriety," the crowds drawn by the report making it hard "to accommodate them, or so as to excite the opposition of civil rulers."

Theophylact (under v.40) reads the leper's act charitably: "although the Lord forbade him, [he] disclosed the benefit... for the person benefited ought to be grateful, and to return thanks, even though his benefactor requires it not." Henry (under v.40) is more cautious: "the leper ought to have observed his orders; yet, no doubt, it was with a good design that he proclaimed the cure," its only ill effect being to so increase the crowds "that he could no more openly enter into the city... because the crowd was so great, that the streets would not hold them."

The Latin fathers read Christ's retirement spiritually. Bede: "even after working a miracle in that city, the Lord retires into the desert, to shew that He loves best a quiet life, and one far removed from the cares of the world." Pseudo-Jerome: "Jesus is not manifested to those who are enslaved to the love of praise in the broad highway... but to those who with Peter go into the desert... those who quit the pleasures of the world." Bede adds that "this perfect cure of one man brought large multitudes to the Lord."

Henry draws the closing reflection: "This shows how expedient it was for us, that Christ should go away, and send the Comforter, for his bodily presence could be but in one place at a time... but by his spiritual presence he is with his people wherever they are." The witnesses agree: the cleansed leper, overcome with gratitude, disregarded Christ's charge and spread the news so widely that Christ had to withdraw to desert places, yet even there the crowds sought him out from every quarter. The chapter thus closes with Christ's fame at its height and his ministry pressed by multitudes — a man's well-meant but disobedient zeal both magnifying the cure and complicating the Master's work.
